stories.title: Citi Prices Largest Single Bank-Contributed, Multi-Only CMBS Conduit Deal Since GFC
stories.excerpt: Dog days of summer? Not for Citigroup, which just priced a record-setting deal. The bank this week priced the largest multifamily-only conduit commercial mortgage-backed securities (CMBS) transaction originated by a single bank since the Global Financial Crisis. The transaction, Citigroup Commercial Mortgage Trust 2026-MFAM1, is an all-multifamily conduit deal comprising 27 five-year, interest-only loans on 27 properties. It has an aggregate principal balance of $816.9 million, and 100 percent of the loans were contributed by Citi Real Estate Funding . In addition to the deal being the biggest single bank-originated multifamily-only conduit deal since the GFC, according to Trepp data, it also seemingly represents the largest loan contribution in a conduit deal by a single bank post-Covid. In 2016, the GSMS 2016-GS4 transaction was a single bank weighing in at a little over $1.2 billion. Further back, J.P. Morgan was the single mortgage loan seller in two conduit deals it issued in 2011: the $1.5 billion JPMCC 2011-C3 and $1.47 billion JPMCC 2011-C4 transactions. The Citigroup Commercial Mortgage Trust 2026-MFAM1 AAA bonds priced at swaps plus 80 basis points (bps) earlier this week, 8 bps tighter than the last cash-flow AAA tranche priced on the JPMF1 Multifamily Mortgage Trust 2026-FX1 transaction, which priced in May. In the latter deal, which was also 100 percent multifamily, MF1 Capital was the mortgage seller for 100 percent of the loans, which totaled $734 million. Sources familiar with the Citi execution said the bank saw a void — and appetite — in the market for medium- to high-leverage loans on high-quality multifamily product, and leaned in. The bank faced some inventory risk with the pool but fully committed to the program, holding terms on every loan’s execution, and everything went swimmingly as a result. According to Fitch Ratings, the Citi deal’s average Fitch loan-to-value (LTV) of 123.4 percent is higher than five-year multiborrower averages in both
bullets: Citi priced $816.9M multifamily-only CMBS, largest single-bank conduit since 2008 GFC.
27 five-year interest-only loans on 27 properties; 100% Citi Real Estate Funding originated.
Signals return of bank appetite for structured multifamily debt after post-pandemic pullback.
stories.summary: Citigroup priced a $816.9 million multifamily-only CMBS conduit (2026-MFAM1) comprising 27 five-year interest-only loans—the largest single-bank originated multifamily conduit deal since the Global Financial Crisis. All loans were contributed by Citi Real Estate Funding. The deal also marks the largest single-bank loan contribution post-Covid, signaling renewed capital market appetite for multifamily debt.
stories.impact: Bank re-entry into multifamily CMBS structuring and syndication lowers refinance costs for seasoned multifamily assets and validates sponsor leverage strategies. Expect tightened spreads on comparable multifamily debt products, improving execution certainty for portfolio refinancings and bridge-to-perm strategies across major markets.

stories.title: South Florida Retail Markets Show Sub-5% Vacancy
stories.excerpt: Despite oft-repeated fears of retail’s pandemic-drive demise, South Florida’s retail sector is enjoying a combination of low vacancy rates, resilient rental rates and robust leasing and investment sales. That’s according to market reports released Thursday by commercial real estate brokerage Colliers. In Miami-Dade County, the retail market experienced net absorption of 378,795 square feet, driving the retail vacancy rate down to 3 percent in the second quarter of 2026, the lowest among the three South Florida counties. Average asking rents in Miami-Dade County rose to $42.50 per square foot per year, up 2.6 percent from the first quarter. More than 1.3 million square feet of retail space are under construction, Colliers reported. Floor & Decor Outlets of America signed the largest lease of the quarter with 55,146 square feet at Park Plaza Shoppes in Kendall. Planet Fitness followed with a 26,207-square-foot deal at 1501 Biscayne Boulevard in Miami. The biggest sale was a $210 million purchase at Miami WorldCenter by a joint venture involving Falcone Group, The Davis Companies and Jamestown. In Broward County, net absorption in the second quarter was a negative 102,436 square feet, causing the retail vacancy rate to increase slightly to 4.3 percent in the second quarter, up from 4.2 percent in the first quarter. Average asking rents in Broward fell to $27.67 per square foot, a 3.4 percent decline from the second quarter of 2025. Wayfair signed Broward County’s largest lease with a new 94,000-square-foot deal at Galleria Mall in Fort Lauderdale, followed by Apogee Lauderdale taking 29,309 square feet at the International Swimming Hall of Fame. In Palm Beach County, net absorption totaled 148,769 square feet during the second quarter, nudging the retail vacancy rate down to 4 percent. Average asking rents increased to $30.14 per square foot, an annual gain of 3.4 percent. Premier Indoor Pickleball led the quarter for Palm Beach County from the tenant side by commi
bullets: Miami-Dade retail vacancy 3%, lowest among Florida counties; Q2 net absorption +378,795 SF.
Average asking rents rose 2.6% QoQ to $42.50/SF; 1.3M SF under construction.
Floor & Decor, Planet Fitness signed largest Q2 leases; $210M sale at Miami WorldCenter.
stories.summary: Miami-Dade County's retail market posted a 3% vacancy rate in Q2 2026 (lowest in three South Florida counties) with net absorption of 378,795 square feet. Average asking rents climbed 2.6% to $42.50/SF year-over-year. Over 1.3 million square feet are under construction, with large-format tenants (Floor & Decor, Planet Fitness) and institutional buyers (Miami WorldCenter sale, $210M) driving momentum.
stories.impact: Strong retail leasing fundamentals in Miami-Dade signal robust consumer demand and tenant confidence in South Florida growth. For multifamily developers, tight retail vacancy and rising rent confirms healthy mixed-use and lifestyle amenity demand; retail-anchored multifamily projects in submarkets like Kendall and Biscayne Corridor are better positioned for pre-leasing and value-add conversions.

stories.title: Gale Associates to Lead Rehab of Mansfield Municipal Airport Runway
stories.excerpt: Gale Associates, Inc., through its Airport Engineering & Planning Group, is providing engineering design and construction administration services for the rehabilitation of Mansfield Municipal Airport’s primary airfield infrastructure. The rehabilitation marks the first reconstruction of the Mansfield, MA airport’s primary runway since 1993. The project includes reconstruction of Runway 14-32, Taxiway A, and the connecting stub taxiways, along with Taxiways B, C, D edge lighting to meet current Federal Aviation Administration pavement design standards. Additional improvements include upgrades to the airport’s drainage system, installation of new airfield electrical infrastructure, guidance signs, runway and taxiway lighting, navigational aids, a new electrical vault, pavement markings and site restoration. Although fixed-wing operations will be temporarily suspended during construction, which is expected to take three months, Boston MedFlight will continue operating from the airport, ensuring uninterrupted emergency medical transport services for communities throughout Southern Massachusetts and Rhode Island, according to Gale Associates. The post Gale Associates to Lead Rehab of Mansfield Municipal Airport Runway appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Mansfield Municipal Airport (MA) primary runway first reconstruction since 1993.
Gale Associates leading 3-month rehabilitation of Runway 14-32 and taxiways.
Minimal impact to multifamily demand or supply fundamentals in market.
stories.summary: Gale Associates is providing engineering and construction administration for the rehabilitation of Mansfield Municipal Airport's primary runway and taxiways in Massachusetts—the first major reconstruction since 1993. The project will take three months and includes FAA infrastructure upgrades.
stories.impact: Limited direct relevance to multifamily development. Airport rehabilitation may marginally improve regional accessibility and business travel convenience, but does not signal material job growth, population inflow, or demand catalysts for residential development.

stories.title: New York People and Company News, Week of July 17, 2026
stories.excerpt: PGIM has hired David Blum as managing director overseeing U.S. high-yield real estate credit investments, strengthening the firm’s U.S. real estate debt platform as investor interest in higher-yielding strategies continues to gain momentum. In the newly created role, Blum will focus on high-yield real estate credit, supporting the firm’s ability to deliver flexible, value-driven debt capital solutions for investors and borrowers. He is based in the firm’s New York office. Robert Koontz has joined CBRE as Senior Managing Director, Head of Multifamily Debt Capital Markets within its Debt & Structured Finance team. Koontz, based between Washington D.C. and New York, brings more than 25 years of capital markets and structured finance experience. He spent 17 years at Freddie Mac, most recently as Head of Multifamily Capital Markets. The Durst Organization has appointed Lucas Durst as the company’s CFO. Lucas began his career at Durst more than a decade ago, starting as a finance associate. Most recently, he led the $1.3-billion CMBS financing of One Five One, the $250 million CMBS financing of 1155 AvE. of the Americas, and the $450-million CMBS financing of SVEN. Lucas Durst previously served as director of finance and is a member of the fourth generation of family leaders at the company. Park Madison Partners, a global real estate capital solutions and advisory firm, today announced that Brian Di Salvo, Partner, Head of Capital Advisory, will oversee the continued growth of its Capital Advisory business. A member of the firm’s Executive Committee, Di Salvo will drive this accelerated growth in his expanded role. Mack Real Estate Group (MREG) announced a series of leadership transitions designed to position the firm for its next chapter of growth. Priyanka Garg, who most recently served as Managing Director and Head of Credit Strategies, has been named President of MREG. Michael McGillis, who previously served as MREG’s President, has been appointed Vice Chair
bullets: PGIM hired David Blum as MD for U.S. high-yield real estate credit; expanded debt platform.
CBRE hired Robert Koontz (25y exp, 17y at Freddie Mac) as SVP Multifamily Debt Capital Markets.
Durst CFO Lucas Durst led $1.3B CMBS (One Five One), $450M CMBS (SVEN) financings.
stories.summary: PGIM expanded its real estate debt capabilities by hiring David Blum to lead U.S. high-yield real estate credit strategies. CBRE appointed Robert Koontz (former Freddie Mac Head of Multifamily Capital Markets) as Senior Managing Director of Multifamily Debt Capital Markets. The Durst Organization promoted Lucas Durst to CFO; he recently structured major CMBS financings for flagship properties.
stories.impact: Talent migration from government-sponsored enterprises (Freddie Mac) to private capital and major brokers signals rising institutional appetite for multifamily debt at non-agency terms. Durst's CMBS activity validates sponsor confidence in debt markets; developers should expect tighter execution timelines and more structured underwriting from incoming leadership at PGIM and CBRE.

stories.title: Capital Arrives in the Sunbelt for the Right Multifamily Deals
stories.excerpt: For the past two years, the multifamily market in the Sunbelt states has struggled with record unit deliveries, rising interest rates and softening rent growth. While those factors remain in play, many investors are starting to see opportunities amid supply overhang and sluggish rent growth. Rather than avoiding the region, capital is moving toward well-located properties backed by experienced sponsors and realistic underwriting. Industry experts told Connect CRE that the conversation is shifting from whether to invest in the Sunbelt to where, and at what price. Jeff Rosenfeld “The market has become much more disciplined,” said North River Partners’ Managing Partner Jeff Rosenfeld. “Investors and lenders are taking a much closer look at fundamentals than they did several years ago.” Buying versus Building Multifamily properties built post-pandemic are generally trading at values below what it would cost to build that same product today. This can provide a basis for patient investors who support a post-demand story. But there’s more involved than buying below replacement costs. “It’s buying below replacement cost in a market that can recover,” Rosenfeld said. Investors have been showing interest in Sunbelt assets over the past two years. However, they aren’t going on buying sprees. “Investors are continuing to be selective, and I expect that stance to hold until early next year,” said Garrett Karam, Chief Investment Officer with EMBREY. Capital is Selective The experts said that debt and equity are available for multifamily acquisitions and developments, but it’s undergoing more scrutiny than during the post-pandemic boom. On the equity side, investors are typically contributing larger amounts of capital as loan proceeds decline. According to a Northmarq analysis, transactions that don’t consider today’s financing environment, replacement costs or exit risks can struggle to attract investment. Garrett Karam Likewise, deals purchased at p
bullets: Sunbelt multifamily shifting from avoidance to selective deal-making; capital seeking value.
Buy-below-replacement-cost strategy favored if market can recover; larger sponsor equity required.
Debt and equity available but scrutinized; no buying sprees until early 2027, per experts.
stories.summary: After two years of record deliveries and softening rents, institutional capital is returning to Sunbelt multifamily but with strict discipline. Investors are targeting post-pandemic buildings trading below replacement cost in recovering markets, but equity contributions are larger and loan proceeds lower than in the boom. Experts expect selective investing to persist until early 2027.
stories.impact: Sunbelt oversupply has created a bifurcated market: well-capitalized sponsors with realistic underwriting can acquire stabilized assets at discounts; underfunded or opportunistic developers face capital drought. For developers, this means greenfield projects require exceptional sponsorship credentials, strong pre-leasing, or partnerships with large institutions. Markets with measurable job growth (Austin, Nashville, Phoenix submarkets) will see capital consolidation around market-rate trophy projects.

stories.title: Essex Closes 33-Unit Albany Park Multifamily Transaction
stories.excerpt: Essex Realty Group and Essex Capital Markets announced the closing of Wilson Manor, a 33-unit multifamily property located at 3348 W. Wilson Avenue in Chicago’s Albany Park neighborhood. The property sold for $7.6 million, a $200,000 premium over the listing price. Originally renovated in 2008, Wilson Manor offers a unit mix consisting primarily of two-bedroom apartments, complemented by several larger three- and four-bedroom duplex units. Significant capital improvements include copper plumbing, new electrical service, and updated wiring.Essex Realty Group Principals Jim Darrow, Matt Feo and Jordan Gottlieb, along with Director Abe Eilian, completed the transaction. Financing for the acquisition was arranged by Essex Capital Markets Directors Asher Motew and Quinn Keenan on behalf of the buyer. The team secured a $6.31 million rehabilitation loan with an 80% loan-to-cost structure, a 6.43% interest rate, and two years of interest-only payments. Stay ahead of the multifamily market at Connect Apartments. Gain firsthand insights from industry leaders on capital markets, development, operations, financing, and investment trends while expanding your network with the people making deals happen. See you on September 22! The post Essex Closes 33-Unit Albany Park Multifamily Transaction appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Essex closed 33-unit Wilson Manor (Chicago Albany Park) for $7.6M, $200K above listing.
Acquisition financed at 80% LTC, 6.43% rate, 2 years IO; $6.31M rehab loan.
Class-B multifamily execution in Midwest; in-place rents support loan structure.
stories.summary: Essex Realty Group and Essex Capital Markets closed the acquisition of Wilson Manor, a 33-unit renovated multifamily property in Chicago's Albany Park neighborhood, for $7.6 million. Financing included a $6.31 million rehabilitation loan at 80% loan-to-cost, 6.43% interest rate, and two years of interest-only payments.
stories.impact: Deal demonstrates continued lending appetite for Class B/C multifamily in secondary Midwest markets at reasonable leverage and cost of capital. 6.43% rates and 2-year IO terms are consistent with current market pricing for stabilized secondary assets; developers with similar profiles (smaller portfolios, in-place income) can expect comparable execution if sponsor track record and market fundamentals are sound.

stories.title: JLL Completes Sale of Kansas City MOB Portfolio to Montecito Medical
stories.excerpt: JLL has completed the sale of a fully leased, four-property medical building portfolio in the Greater Kansas City area. JLL worked on behalf of several different physician groups, Orthopedic Health of Kansas City, Kansas City Urology Care PA and Sunflower Medical Group, to complete the off-market sale to Montecito Medical. JLL’s Capital Markets’ Medical Properties group, representing the seller, was led by Senior Managing Directors Evan Kovac, Andrew Milne and John Chun, Managing Director Tim Joyce and Senior Director Matt DiCesare. Executive Vice President Jim Gates in JLL’s Kansas City office provided local market expertise. Totaling 86,135 square feet, the portfolio is fully leased to three Kansas City regional medical practices. “We’re pleased to add this exceptional portfolio to our growing national portfolio of healthcare real estate,” said Chip Conk, CEO of Montecito Medical. “The quality of these physician groups, the strength of their long-term commitment to the market and the mission-critical nature of these facilities make this an outstanding fit for Montecito.” The post JLL Completes Sale of Kansas City MOB Portfolio to Montecito Medical appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: JLL sold four-property medical office portfolio (86,135 SF) in Greater Kansas City to Montecito Medical.
Portfolio fully leased to regional physician groups; off-market transaction.
Minimal impact to multifamily demand or development strategy.
stories.summary: JLL completed the off-market sale of a fully leased, four-property medical office building portfolio (86,135 SF) in Greater Kansas City to Montecito Medical on behalf of three regional physician groups. The MOB focus and off-market structure suggest institutional consolidation of primary-care healthcare assets.
stories.impact: Limited relevance to multifamily development. Medical office transactions indicate healthcare real estate demand but do not directly signal residential population growth, job creation, or renter demand catalysts in Kansas City.

stories.title: Prologis Beats Expectations With Record Leasing and Data Center Growth
stories.excerpt: The world’s biggest warehouse landlord is still full steam ahead on data centers. “After several quarters of sustained demand, we believe the market is entering its next phase,” Prologis CEO Dan Letter said in a second-quarter earnings call Thursday afternoon. The real estate investment trust (REIT) beat expectations in the second quarter of 2026, with record leasing and year-to-date data center starts already surpassing its full-year guidance. “The same land, customer relationships and operating capabilities that have made us the leader in logistics are enabling our data center and energy businesses, creating two additional long-term growth opportunities for Prologis,” Letter said during the call. The quarterly results come just six days before Prologis has a big decision to make. It must either make a formal offer to buy Segro, Britain’s biggest warehouse REIT and its international rival, or abandon its unsolicited bid. Segro’s board firmly rejected Prologis’ $16.6 billion all-share acquisition proposal in June. The company’s executives declined to take questions on the offer, but Letter told attendees that they’d put forward a “very compelling proposal.” “It offers a meaningful premium to where the stock has traded, and values the business above its stated [net tangible assets],” Letter said. “Importantly, it gives Segro shareholders the opportunity to participate in the upside of a stronger combined company, and the value of the Prologis enterprise and everything it offers should not be overlooked.” The news that the company leadership could share, however, was highly positive. Prologis reported core funds from operations (FFO) of $1.56 billion, up from $1.4 billion a year earlier and $1.44 billion last quarter. Net income reached $1.06 billion for the second quarter, nearly doubling from the $570 million reported in the same period of 2025, and up significantly from $980.5 million last quarter. Total revenue also grew annually, from $2.18 billion to $2.43 billi
bullets: Prologis beat Q2 2026 guidance with record leasing; data center starts exceed full-year target.
CEO Dan Letter: market entering next phase; data centers leveraging logistics platforms.
Segro $16.6B acquisition bid rejected June; formal offer decision due imminently.
stories.summary: Prologis reported record second-quarter 2026 leasing and data center starts exceeding full-year guidance. CEO Dan Letter emphasized that the logistics REIT is leveraging its land, customer relationships, and operations to expand into data center and energy businesses. The company faces a decision on whether to formally bid for British REIT Segro ($16.6 billion rejected proposal).
stories.impact: Prologis data center acceleration and logistics strength indicate robust underlying demand for last-mile distribution and technology infrastructure. For multifamily developers in markets with strong industrial corridors (Phoenix, Dallas, Southeast), logistics clustering drives employment density and rental demand; industrial co-location with residential infill projects in distribution hubs (e.g., near DFW, Atlanta) amplifies workforce capture and lease velocity.

stories.title: Live Shopping Marketplace Storefront Takes 5K SF at 555 West 25th Street
stories.excerpt: Storefront, a new e-commerce platform and live shopping marketplace for brands and independent sellers, has signed a 4,700-square-foot lease at Mactaggart Family & Partners’ office building at 555 West 25th Street, Commercial Observer has learned. The length of the lease is for two years and the asking rent was in the high $30s per square foot, according to Michael Segerman from Current Real Estate Advisors, who represented both the landlord and the tenant in the deal. Storefront will launch this fall. “They’re growing very, very fast, and they’re probably going to be expanding within the building at some point as well,” Segerman told CO. Representing both the landlord and tenant also provided his firm the opportunity for greater transparency and equality for everyone involved, Segerman said. “It creates a fair deal for both sides,” he said. “ There are no hidden facts, we just cut to the chase, and it makes it much easier to get deals done.” It’s unclear whether the deal represents a new location or a relocation for Storefront, but it will join another new tenant at the Chelsea building. Susan Rodriguez Architects, an architecture and design firm founded in 2017, recently extended its lease agreement at 555 West 25th Street. The firm will maintain its 4,151-square-foot presence in the building, Segerman said. Cushman & Wakefield brokered that deal on behalf of the tenant. The names of the brokers were not immediately available. C&W did not immediately respond to a request for comment. Amanda Schiavo can be reached at aschiavo@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Storefront (e-commerce/live shopping platform) signed 4,700 SF at 555 West 25th, Chelsea, NYC.
Two-year lease at high $30s/SF; tenant expected to expand within building.
AI and tech startups clustering in West Chelsea office; soft office market conversion signal.
stories.summary: Storefront, a new e-commerce and live shopping platform, leased 4,700 square feet at 555 West 25th Street in Manhattan's Chelsea neighborhood for two years at asking rents in the high $30s per square foot. The broker noted rapid growth and potential for in-building expansion.
stories.impact: Tech/e-commerce tenant activity in Chelsea signals cautious office demand recovery in premium Manhattan submarkets. For multifamily developers, office-to-residential conversions and residential infill near Chelsea's High Line corridor remain viable; young tech workers command higher rents and amenity expectations, validating new construction and luxury repositioning in nearby Meatpacking/Hudson Yards submarkets.

stories.title: AI Firm Isara Laboratories Inks 6K-SF Lease at 515 West 20th Street
stories.excerpt: Isara Laboratories, an artificial intelligence startup platform, has taken 6,009 square feet at the boutique office building at 515 West 20th Street, Commercial Observer has learned. The company, which builds software so AI agents can communicate and solve problems for the finance and biotech industries, signed the lease June 20, according to broker Current Real Estate Advisors. The landlord is Justin Amirian’s Eagle Point Properties, which bought the Chelsea property from A&R Kalimian Realty for $23.3 million in 2022, property records show. The seven-story mixed-use building along the High Line is famously home to members-only club Soho House. Michael Segerman from Current Real Estate Advisors represented both the tenant and the landlord in the deal. “There’s been an AI boom among office tenants,” Segerman told Commercial Observer. “They’re not necessarily signing very, very long-term leases, but they are gravitating toward this West Chelsea pocket. They want something that is downtown and hip, but not priced like the SoHo market.” Sergerman did not share the specific asking rent, but he said office rents at 515 West 20th Street are in the high $30s per square foot. The length of the lease is for two years. Isara had set up a temporary office shop at 43 West 29th Street, which is still listed on its website. The company is in the process of moving into its new office space. A spokesperson for Isara did not immediately respond to CO’s requests for comment. The firm’s “home base” for its engineering team is in London, while its commercial and customer teams are in New York, and its engineering and research hub is in Romania. Built in 1935 and renovated in 1985, 515 West 20th Street is a nearly 84,000-square-foot office property. Other tenants in the building include the Fred Astaire Dance Studio, which signed a 3,258-square-foot office lease at the property on May 1, according to Current Real Estate Advisors. Amanda Schiavo can be reached at aschiavo@commer
bullets: Isara Laboratories (AI startup) leased 6,009 SF at 515 West 20th Street, Chelsea, NYC (June 20).
Boutique mixed-use building; rents below SoHo; AI tenants gravitating to West Chelsea.
Soft office market; startups prefer downtown/hip/lower-cost locations.
stories.summary: Isara Laboratories, an AI software startup serving finance and biotech, signed a 6,009-square-foot lease at 515 West 20th Street (Chelsea, Manhattan) in June 2026. The building, owned by Eagle Point Properties (acquired for $23.3M in 2022), is home to Soho House. Brokers noted an AI boom among office tenants gravitating toward West Chelsea for its downtown cool factor and rents below SoHo.
stories.impact: AI startup clustering in West Chelsea signals demand for lower-cost, lifestyle-oriented office space in Manhattan; this reduces pressure for office conversions in premium submarkets. However, young AI workers remain density-oriented and gravitate toward transit-rich, mixed-use neighborhoods, supporting residential infill near Chelsea's Meatpacking and Hudson Yards submarkets—developers should prioritize transit connectivity and neighborhood amenities over pure space.

stories.title: Miami secures $193M permit to build civic center at Freedom Park
stories.excerpt: The city of Miami’s new civic center, which will replace an aging administrative building downtown and the city’s iconic city hall in Coconut Grove, secured a $193 million construction permit, the city confirmed. The city received its permit July 1,  just over a week before construction workers topped off the eight-story, 257,000-square-foot building on July 10. Adler Development is the developer, and Moss Construction is the general contractor.  The building will be part of the 130-acre Miami Freedom Park development, which is a $1 billion project anchored by Inter Miami CF’s stadium near Miami International Airport. The project, which includes […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Miami civic center (8-story, 257K SF) secured $193M permit; construction topped off July 10.
Part of $1B Miami Freedom Park (130 acres); Inter Miami CF stadium anchor; completion spring 2028.
$20M pedestrian bridge from Metrorail; consolidates city admin from downtown and Coconut Grove.
stories.summary: The City of Miami secured a $193 million construction permit for its new civic center (257,000 SF, eight stories) at Miami Freedom Park on July 1; construction topped off July 10. Adler Development and Moss Construction are leading the project. The building is part of the $1 billion, 130-acre mixed-use Miami Freedom Park development anchored by Inter Miami CF's stadium near Miami International Airport, with a $20 million Metrorail pedestrian bridge. Completion targeted for spring 2028.
stories.impact: Miami Freedom Park's scale and Metrorail connectivity will drive significant multifamily and mixed-use development around the site and along the airport-to-downtown corridor. Developers should acquire land and entitlements near the Metrorail bridge to capture live-work-play demand from Inter Miami fans, airport workers, and civic employees. The 2028 completion timeline supports near-term ground-lease or development partner opportunities in a high-visibility sports/civic district.

stories.title: Santa Monica, elevated: Related sets new standard for SoCal luxury with lifestyle amenities, Thermador appliances
stories.excerpt: Today’s luxury buyer isn’t just looking for fancy finishes and great views; they’re expecting a residence that facilitates their lifestyle. In our latest Developer Diary, TRD visited 700 Broadway and sat down with Related California’s Peter Garofalo to learn how the developer created an exclusive luxury residential experience in one of SoCal’s most desirable submarkets. “The ideal resident for 700 Broadway is someone who wants to have it all in a wonderfully designed and luxurious setting,” said Garofalo. “Every space has been perfectly designed to keep a balance between the beachside ease and the city’s bustling energy.” With nearly 70,000 […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Related's 700 Broadway (Santa Monica) emphasizes lifestyle amenities; 70K SF indoor/outdoor.
Three-building development; private 3/4-acre courtyard; rooftop pools, Speakeasy bar, curated design.
Signals luxury multifamily shift toward experiential/wellness over finishes alone; SoCal prime.
stories.summary: Related California's 700 Broadway in Santa Monica features nearly 70,000 square feet of curated amenity space across three buildings, a private three-quarter-acre courtyard, rooftop pools and hot tubs, a Speakeasy-style bar, and handmade natural materials. Developer Peter Garofalo emphasized a design philosophy balancing 'beachside ease' with urban energy to create an 'entire outdoor recreation resort' and lifestyle experience.
stories.impact: 700 Broadway exemplifies the premium shift toward experiential, wellness-focused amenities and designer curated spaces in luxury coastal markets. For developers in premier SoCal, NYC, and South Florida submarkets, amenity investment (70K+ SF per project) and lifestyle programming are now table-stakes; generic fitness centers and lounges will not command luxury rent premiums. Projects emphasizing resident experience, outdoor recreation, and biophilic design command higher absorption and rental growth in trophy markets.

stories.title: Moratorium on waterfront project applications in downtown West Palm heads to final vote
stories.excerpt: West Palm Beach commissioners will take a final vote on pausing new project applications for high-rise waterfront developments at next week’s meeting.  The proposed ordinance would create a six-month moratorium on new planned development applications for properties south of Monroe Drive, north of Southern Boulevard, west of Flagler Drive and east of Washington Road, according to the commission agenda. Commissioners approved it on first reading at a meeting last week; a second reading vote is scheduled for July 20.  The city cited rapid transformation of that corridor of apartment and condo buildings and is hiring Zyscovich to complete a zoning […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: West Palm Beach imposes 6-month moratorium (effective Jan 20, 2027) on high-rise waterfront projects.
Flagler corridor south of Monroe Drive targeted; rapid condo/apartment growth cited.
12+ luxury condo projects (2K+ units) in development; Related, Terra, BH Group active.
stories.summary: West Palm Beach commissioners approved a first reading of an ordinance imposing a six-month moratorium on new planned development applications for high-rise waterfront properties in the Flagler corridor (south of Monroe Drive, north of Southern Boulevard). A final vote is scheduled for July 20. The pause expires January 20, 2027, unless extended. The city hired Zyscovich to conduct zoning and economic analysis and draft new regulations. More than a dozen luxury condo projects totaling over 2,000 units are in development, with Miami-based developers (Related Group, Terra, BH Group) leading.
stories.impact: West Palm Beach's moratorium signals overdevelopment concerns and regulatory tightening after rapid condo growth. Apartment developers should expect revised zoning, density caps, and extended approval timelines post-January 2027. Existing projects with entitlements should expedite permitting; new greenfield projects will face higher scrutiny and may require community benefits or mixed-income components. Moratorium may shift capital to adjacent markets (Fort Lauderdale, Doral) with friendlier entitlements.

stories.title: MidPen floats 110-unit affordable housing project on former Wells Fargo site in Sunnyvale
stories.excerpt: Developer interest in downtown Sunnyvale continues with another multifamily project proposal as more tech and artificial intelligence workers flock to the Silicon Valley city.  Foster City-based nonprofit MidPen Housing is planning a 110-unit affordable housing building at 295 South Mathilda Avenue in Sunnyvale, the East Bay Times reported. All of the units, save for a market-rate manager’s unit, will be income restricted.  The development was proposed at a former Wells Fargo bank site, which would give way to the residential building. The development earned support from some residents and community groups who claim it “will assist the city by better […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: MidPen Housing proposing 110-unit affordable housing at 295 South Mathilda (former Wells Fargo site), Sunnyvale.
All units income-restricted except manager's unit; 25% for previously unhoused; AI/tech demand driving interest.
Beam Reach proposed 265-unit residential at 215 Bordeaux (former Google offices); planning approved.
stories.summary: Sunnyvale-based nonprofit MidPen Housing is planning a 110-unit affordable housing building at 295 South Mathilda Avenue, a former Wells Fargo bank site. All units except a manager's unit will be income-restricted, with 25% designated for previously unhoused individuals. Developer interest in downtown Sunnyvale is accelerating as AI and tech workers drive housing demand. Dallas-based Beam Reach separately proposed a 265-unit residential project via office-to-residential conversion at 215 Bordeaux Drive (former Google space), with planning approval.
stories.impact: Sunnyvale is becoming a critical satellite market to San Francisco as AI/tech employment bleeds out from downtown SF. Office-to-residential conversions (Beam Reach 265 units) and new affordable housing (MidPen 110 units) signal strong demand fundamentals and developer confidence. Apartment developers should prioritize Sunnyvale land acquisitions, CEQA fast-track paths, and infill sites near Caltrain; build-to-rent and mixed-income projects will be favored over luxury-only. Expect rents to rise sharply as local supply lags tech hiring.

stories.title: Pair of Frisco entrepreneurs ordered to pay $400M in damages to Georgetown business over alleged fraud scheme
stories.excerpt: Frisco entrepreneurs Gopala Krishnan and Sakthivel Gounder were ordered to pay $400 million in damages to a Georgetown-based business after being found liable for breach of contract and fraud by a Williamson County jury.  The pair concocted promotional materials for a luxury development called “The District” that they claimed was going to be built around the San Gabriel River. Krishnan and Gounder were sold the land by the company that sued them, Energy Commissioning Incorporated, owned by Marshall Hussain.  Energy Commissioning was subsequently hired by the pair for $21 million to build necessary infrastructure for a 400-acre, luxury waterfront development […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Frisco entrepreneurs Krishnan and Gounder ordered to pay $400M damages for fraud; swindled Georgetown business owner.
Fabricated 'The District' luxury development ($5B bond scheme); concocted promotional materials.
No material impact to multifamily fundamentals; litigation and developer credibility risk.
stories.summary: A Williamson County jury found Frisco entrepreneurs Gopala Krishnan and Sakthivel Gounder liable for breach of contract and fraud, ordering them to pay $400 million in damages. The pair defrauded Georgetown-based Energy Commissioning Inc. (owned by Marshall Hussain) over a fictitious 400-acre 'The District' waterfront development. They solicited $21 million in infrastructure payments and attempted a $5 billion bond raise via false promotional materials. Hussain lost his home as a result. The defendants were previously targeted by the SEC in 2023.
stories.impact: Limited direct impact to multifamily fundamentals. However, the fraud scheme and $400M judgment underscore importance of sponsor diligence, financial transparency, and legal/regulatory compliance for multifamily developers and lenders. Projects in Texas dealing with these individuals or their affiliated entities should perform enhanced due diligence; this case illustrates reputational and capital access risks from fraud.

stories.title: Bellhops and bass fishing: Wildhaven developer bets big on nature-centric luxury in Denton
stories.excerpt: A planned luxury resort might be coming to Denton, complete with six miles of horse trails to help bring residents and visitors closer to nature.  Sarasota, Florida-based An Ki Land Development’s managing director, Logan Breen, told the Dallas Business Journal that the new resort they plan on building is intended to combine upscale homes and hotels with “authentic outdoor experiences,” with a total value between $350 and $450 million when completed. The resort, called Wildhaven, is set to span 740 acres on the north side of Denton. 152 homes are set to be built in the resort, ranging from $1.5 […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: An Ki Land Development (Sarasota) planning Wildhaven luxury resort (740 acres) in Denton, TX; $350-450M value.
152 homes ($1.5M-$3M+), 100-key hotel, equestrian, spa, bass pond; 400 of 740 acres developed.
First Wildhaven branded resort; follows Denton/DFW population and development boom.
stories.summary: An Ki Land Development (based in Sarasota, Florida) is planning Wildhaven, a 740-acre luxury resort development in Denton, Texas, with a projected value of $350–$450 million upon completion. The project includes 152 homes ranging from $1.5 million to over $3 million, a 100-key hotel, equestrian facilities, spa, restaurants, and a 10-acre bass fishing pond. HKS is the design firm; Noble Classic Homes will build homes on 20 initial lots. More than 300 of the 740 acres will be preserved for natural space. Wildhaven is the flagship project of An Ki's branded resort strategy, with planned expansions in Colorado and Florida.
stories.impact: Wildhaven reflects confidence in Denton's population and economic growth trajectory within the DFW corridor. The project's large acreage, master-planned scope, and lifestyle/resort positioning indicate developer expectations of strong demand for premium residential and hospitality in North Texas. For competing multifamily developers, Wildhaven's entry signals that destination lifestyle living is a viable category in secondary DFW markets; mixed-use residential projects incorporating resort/wellness amenities and hotel integration will capture affluent households and part-time residents in Denton and Frisco.
